story.title: Introducing Parallel Agents in Zed - Zed Blog

comments.text:
Interesting timing on this. We've been building something similar at Cursor, and the parallel agents + workspace isolation is definitely where the tooling needs to go. The threading model here looks clean.

One thing I'd be curious about: how does Zed handle agent conflicts when multiple threads are editing the same file? We've found this is where most agentic workflows break down in practice. The Threads Sidebar makes it *visible* that you have 3 agents touching the same codebase, but orchestrating writes without merge conflicts or corrupted state is the hard part.

Also worth noting that VS Code's extension ecosystem has a few contenders here now too (including some paid options that handle multi-agent orchestration). The 120 fps rendering is genuinely nice and Zed's latency story is real, but most developers aren't context-switching between agents fast enough that frame rate becomes the bottleneck.

That said, if you're already in Zed, keeping agents native to the editor instead of shelling out to separate processes is the right call. The UX wins from having everything in one window are substantial. Open-sourcing it is solid too; most teams building agent workflows will have highly specific requirements anyway.

kids.text:
The file handling piece is definitely the crux. We went down a similar path at Cursor and landed on a pretty different model than what I'd expect Zed is doing here.

Instead of trying to orchestrate writes at the editor level, we started treating the workspace more like a git branch system. Each agent thread gets its own isolated view of the codebase, and conflicts surface as merge-like diffs that the user explicitly resolves. Sounds clunkier than it is, but it actually prevents the weird state where two agents have divergent views of what's in a file.

The Threads Sidebar approach of making visibility the core feature is solid though. Most teams we talk to just want to know "okay, what is actually happening right now across all my agents?" before they care about preventing conflicts. So showing active threads per file is a useful intermediate step.

Curious if Zed is doing full file locking or something more optimistic. The "120 fps" thing suggests they're not doing synchronous coordination on every keystroke, which is the right call. But then the question becomes: what happens when agent A writes lines 40-50 and agent B writes lines 45-60 while both are mid-flight?

The file conflict problem is real, but I think you're overweighting it. We've shipped something similar at my last gig and found that in practice, agent isolation by file or directory solves 80% of the pain. Most agentic workflows naturally separate concerns (one agent on tests, one on implementation, one on docs). The visible threading in Zed's Sidebar actually helps here-you can see exactly who's touching what. The hard case is true concurrent edits, but that's rare if your prompt instructions are even slightly thoughtful about scope.

The file conflict problem is actually more subtle than directory isolation suggests. You're describing a locking model that works for sequential workflows, but Zed's parallel execution means you need something closer to operational transformation or CRDT semantics if agents are genuinely concurrent. Figuring out merge semantics for code (not just detecting conflicts) is where tools like this usually hit a wall. See the Teletype for Atom postmortem from a few years back on this exact issue-they solved visibility but never cracked the merge story cleanly.

comments.text: I remember when we had to choose: one agent at a time, one window at a time. Now Zed's running parallel agents in a single viewport, and it feels almost quaint that we used to think sequentially about this. The 120 fps constraint is the real tell here - most tools would have buckled under the UI overhead. This is the kind of polish that separates the "technically works" from the "actually ships."

kids.text: I wonder if this is just feature creep dressed up as innovation. "Parallel agents" sounds great until you're debugging why three agents stepped on each other's toes in the same codebase. The 120 fps thing feels like a distraction from the actual hard problem: making multi-agent coordination reliable.

The reliability concern is real, but "stepping on each other's toes" assumes they're all writing to the same files simultaneously. Zed's model here is that you're controlling which worktrees and folders each agent thread can access via the Threads Sidebar. That's the actual coordination mechanism, not some magic multi-agent consensus layer.

The gotcha is that most people will assume this works like running `npm install` and `cargo build` in parallel on the same repo (great, saves time). But the docs don't really spell out what happens if you point two agents at overlapping file paths and they both decide to edit the same function. That coordination story is still hand-wavy. The 120 fps performance is genuinely impressive, but it's orthogonal to whether the tool prevents or handles conflicts gracefully. You could have the smoothest UI in the world and still ship corrupted code if agent A and agent B both write to the same line.

comments.text:
We built something similar at Stripe's internal tooling a few years back, and the lesson we learned was that parallel agents need ruthless isolation or they'll step on each other's toes constantly. The filesystem writes are the easy part-it's the mental model that matters. When you have three agents modifying the same file simultaneously, you either need transactions (impossible in practice) or you need to make it visually obvious what's happening.

The Threads Sidebar approach here looks solid. Grouping by project and letting users pick which agent accesses what is the right call. The 120 fps claim is interesting though-I'd want to see how that holds up once you have 5+ threads with real LLM latency variance. Zed's rendering is genuinely smooth, but I suspect the bottleneck shifts to agent scheduling once you hit scale.

One thing I don't see mentioned: what happens when two agents are in the same worktree and one gets rate-limited? Does the other keep going? Do they queue? That's usually where this breaks in practice.

kids.text:
This is a good observation, but I'd push back slightly on the "transactions are impossible" framing. Stripe's internal tooling probably had constraints that made true MVCC impractical, but Zed's architecture here seems deliberately designed around the file watching / diff model rather than trying to achieve perfect consistency.

The key insight isn't isolation per se-it's that agents need to be aware of concurrent modifications, not unaware of them. If agent A and agent B are both editing the same file, the worst case isn't a corruption, it's that agent B's context gets stale. Zed seems to handle this by making the edit history visible in the Threads Sidebar, which is honestly more honest about what's happening than pretending you have transactions.

What'd be interesting to see is how they handle git merge conflicts when two agents push to the same branch simultaneously. That's usually where the "step on toes" problem actually bites.

MVCC without true transactions is still a problem in practice. I remember reading about this in the Sublime Merge docs - they had to add explicit conflict markers because even "awareness" of concurrent edits doesn't solve the merge problem when both agents are making semantic changes. Zed's diff model helps visualization but doesn't prevent agent A from undoing agent B's work if they're operating on overlapping regions. Worth watching how this evolves.

comments.text: I think the framing here is backwards. Instead of "parallel agents in one window," this is really about workspace isolation and permission boundaries becoming the primary UI concern. The Threads Sidebar isn't novel because it runs agents side-by-side, it's novel because you can finally say "agent A gets access to /src/auth, agent B gets /src/api, agent C gets nothing." That's the actual problem being solved. The parallelism is almost incidental. Most IDEs treat the entire codebase as one flat namespace to any tool. Once you invert that and make sandboxing the first-class concept, the multi-agent orchestration falls out naturally.

kids.text: You nailed it. The permission model is the real win here. I've been burned before by agents with access to the entire repo-one bad prompt and you're watching it refactor your auth layer. Being able to sandbox agent A to just the UI package while agent B works on API contracts makes this actually usable at scale. The 120 fps smoothness is nice marketing, but the sidebar UI for managing those boundaries is what makes it production-ready.

I'd push back slightly-the permission model is nice, but "production-ready" assumes your agents respect boundaries, which they often don't. A single prompt like "refactor this entire service for performance" can make an agent ignore scope constraints when it thinks it's optimizing. Has anyone here actually tested whether Zed's sandboxing holds up under adversarial prompting, or is this more of a "polite agent" assumption?

comments.text:
Installed the latest Zed build this morning and ran the parallel agents setup with Claude on a medium-sized codebase (about 40K LOC spread across 5 projects). The performance claim holds up-I didn't see any frame drops even with two agents running simultaneous refactors in different worktrees. The Threads Sidebar isolation actually matters in practice; one agent getting stuck or hitting a rate limit no longer blocks the other.

One caveat: agent-to-agent coordination is still manual. If you want them working on related changes, you need to manage the dependency yourself. Not a blocker, but worth knowing before you spin up three agents expecting them to orchestrate intelligently.

kids.text: This is making me curious about Zed's actual performance under load compared to VS Code with the Aider extension, which does something similar. Anyone benchmarked that?

I've used both extensively. Aider+VS Code is still faster for single-agent work on large codebases (the language server overhead in Zed is noticeable past 100K LOC), but the parallel execution is genuinely useful when you're coordinating multiple transformations. VS Code doesn't have an equivalent yet. The real question is whether Zed's agent isolation actually prevents the cascade failures you hit with sequential Aider runs.
